messages.content: okay last time we we rebased llama.cpp and put buun-llama over it. i want to make that process a skill. want to get the latest llama.cpp repo and put buun-llama over it. then we can makethat process a skill. i then want to change the daily llama.cpp cron job to call the skill we make to keep both llama.cpp and buun-llama up to date. so that when the modelis ever restarted its brand new. to be clear the current directory ~/buun-llama-cpp/ is what we want updated. buun-llama and the llama.cpp it forked should be updated to latest daily by the cron script
Let me first inspect the current state of everything — the buun repo, existing cron job, and any sync scripts.

#!/bin/bash

# llama-server start script with detection and timestamped log filename
# Checks if server is already running, shows output in terminal AND logs to file
BASE_DIR="/mnt/g/models/bartowski/Qwen_Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-GGUF"
LOG_DIR="${BASE_DIR}/logs"
MODEL="Qwen_Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-IQ4_XS"
LOG_BASE="${LOG_DIR}/buun-server_${MODEL}"
TIMESTAMP=$(date '+%Y-%m-%d_%H-%M-%S')
LOG_FILE="${LOG_BASE}_${TIMESTAMP}.log"

mkdir -p "$LOG_DIR"

echo "Checking if buun-llama-server is already running on port 8081..."

# Check if llama-server is already running on port 8081
if pgrep -f "llama-server.*--port 8081" > /dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "✓ llama-server is already running on port 8081"
    echo "PID: $(pgrep -f 'llama-server.*--port 8081')"
    exit 0", "exit_code": 0, "error": null}

messages.content:
{"output": "#!/bin/bash
# Daily llama.cpp build with resource limits
# Runs automatically via cron

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
LOG_FILE="$HOME/.hermes/llama-server/daily-build.log"

echo "========================================" >> "$LOG_FILE"
echo "$(date): Starting daily llama.cpp build" >> "$LOG_FILE"
echo "========================================" >> "$LOG_FILE"

# Mount I: drive if needed
if ! mountpoint -q /mnt/i 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "Mounting I: drive..." >> "$LOG_FILE"
    sudo -n mount -t drvfs I: /mnt/i >> "$LOG_FILE" 2>&1
fi

# Check if mount succeeded
if ! mountpoint -q /mnt/i 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "ERROR: Failed to mount I: drive" >> "$LOG_FILE"
    exit 1
fi

# Add safe directory for git
git config --global --add safe.directory /mnt/i/Downloads/llama.cpp 2>/dev/null

# Update repo
echo "Updating llama.cpp repository..." >> "$LOG_FILE"
cd /mnt/i/Downloads/llama.cpp || exit 1
git fetch origin >> "$LOG_FILE" 2>&1

{"output": "#!/bin/bash

# llama-server start script with detection and timestamped log filename
# Checks if server is already running, shows output in terminal AND logs to file
BASE_DIR="/mnt/g/models/bartowski/Qwen_Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-GGUF"
LOG_DIR="${BASE_DIR}/logs"
MODEL="Qwen_Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-IQ4_XS"
LOG_BASE="${LOG_DIR}/buun-server_${MODEL}"
TIMESTAMP=$(date '+%Y-%m-%d_%H-%M-%S')
LOG_FILE="${LOG_BASE}_${TIMESTAMP}.log"

mkdir -p "$LOG_DIR"

echo "Checking if buun-llama-server is already running on port 8081..."

# Check if llama-server is already running on port 8081
if pgrep -f "llama-server.*--port 8081" > /dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "✓ llama-server is already running on port 8081"
    echo "PID: $(pgrep -f 'llama-server.*--port 8081')"
    exit 0
fi

echo "Starting buun-llama-server..."
echo "Log file: $LOG_FILE"
echo "================================"

# Run llama-server, tee to terminal and timestamped log
# Use ts to add timestamps to the log file
~/buun-llama-cpp/build/bin/llama-server \
  --ctx-size 262144 \
  --n-predict 32768 \
  --batch-size 2048 \
  --ubatch-size 1024 \
  --flash-attn on \
  --cache-type-k turbo4 \
  --cache-type-v turbo4 \
  --no-mmap \
  --n-gpu-layers all \
  --model "${BASE_DIR}/${MODEL}.gguf" \
  --log-file "$LOG_FILE" \
  --log-colors on \
  --offline \
  --log-prefix \
  --log-timestamps \
  --temperature 1.0 \
  --top-k 20 \
  --top-p 0.95 \
  --min-p 0.00 \
  --repeat-penalty 1.0 \
  --presence-penalty 1.5 \
  --ctx-checkpoints 16 \
  --cache-ram 4096 \
  --kv-unified \
  --parallel 2 \
  --mmproj "${BASE_DIR}/mmproj-Qwen_Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-f16.gguf" \
  --image-min-tokens 1024 \
  --host 0.0.0.0 \
  --port 8081 \
  --api-key dummythicc \
  --props \
  --reasoning on \
  --chat-template-kwargs '{"preserve_thinking":"on"}'

---

/home/zg/buun-server_qwen.5-27B-test.sh
/home/zg/buun-server_qwen.5-27B.sh

{"output": "name:	gkraker04/buun-llama-cpp
description:	Clean fork of spiritbuun/buun-llama-cpp
--
# buun-llama-cpp

![buunslamma](buunslamma.png)

> **This is a highly experimental fork of llama.cpp. Use at your own discretion.**

A fork of [llama.cpp](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp) with **Trellis-Coded Quantization (TCQ)** for KV cache compression. 2-3x more context in the same VRAM, with quality that matches or beats FP16.

**Paper**: [Closing the Gap: Trellis-Coded Quantization for KV Cache at 2-3 Bits](https://huggingface.co/datasets/spiritbuun/turboquant-tcq-kv-cache)

## What is TCQ?

Standard KV cache quantization treats each value independently. TCQ constrains quantization indices to follow a 512-state trellis, enabling a much larger effective codebook at the same bit rate. Combined with FWHT rotation and context-adaptive norm scaling, this achieves **10-44%!K(MISSING)L-divergence reduction** over scalar quantization at 2-3 bits per value.

At 3.25 bits per value, TCQ produces **lower perplexity than FP16** KV cache (5.802 vs 5.805).

## Build

```sh
cmake -B build \
  -DGGML_CUDA=ON \
  -DGGML_NATIVE=ON \
  -DGGML_CUDA_FA=ON \
  -DGGML_CUDA_FA_ALL_QUANTS=ON \
  -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release

cmake --build build -j$(nproc)
```

## Recommended configurations

### turbo4 (4.25 bpv) -- lossless quality, great compression

The safe default. Virtually no quality loss vs FP16 with ~3.8x KV cache compression and no speed penalty.

```sh
./build/bin/llama-server -m model.gguf -ngl 99 -fa \
  -ctk turbo4 -ctv turbo4
```

### 3-bit TCQ (3.25 bpv) -- best quality at 3-bit

Beats FP16 quality at short context, stays within 2%!a(MISSING)t long context. ~5x KV cache compression.

```sh
./build/bin/llama-server -m model.gguf -ngl 99 -fa \
  -ctk turbo3_tcq -ctv turbo3_tcq
```

### 2-bit TCQ (2.25 bpv) -- maximum compression

~7x KV cache compression. Best for fitting very long contexts in limited VRAM.

```sh
./build/bin/llama-server -m model.gguf -ngl 99 -fa \
  -ctk turbo2_tcq -ctv turbo2_tcq
```

### Asymmetric 2.75 bpv -- best 2-bit quality

3-bit keys + 2-bit values. 15-17%!l(MISSING)ower KLD than the reverse, because adaptive alpha already compensates V quantization error.

```sh
./build/bin/llama-server -m model.gguf -ngl 99 -fa \
  -ctk turbo3_tcq -ctv turbo2_tcq
```

### Scalar turbo3 / turbo2 (3.25 / 2.25 bpv) -- no trellis

Scalar quantization without TCQ. Faster encode, worse quality than TCQ equivalents.

```sh
# 3-bit scalar
./build/bin/llama-server -m model.gguf -ngl 99 -fa \
  -ctk turbo3 -ctv turbo3

# 2-bit scalar
./build/bin/llama-server -m model.gguf -ngl 99 -fa \
  -ctk turbo2 -ctv turbo2
```

## Quality (KL-divergence, Qwen3.5-27B Q6_K, RTX 3090)

Lower is better. Measured against FP16 KV cache base logits.

| Config | bpv | KLD @2K | KLD @7K |
|--------|-----|---------|---------|
| turbo3_tcq (symmetric) | 3.25 | 0.058 | 0.074 |
| turbo3_tcq-K / turbo2_tcq-V | 2.75 | 0.078 | 0.101 |
| turbo2_tcq (symmetric) | 2.25 | 0.101 | 0.136 |

3-bit TCQ at 2K context achieves **lower perplexity than FP16** (5.802 vs 5.805) due to a mild regularizing effect from norm scaling.

## Speed (Qwen3.5-27B Q6_K, RTX 3090)

| Config | Decode (tg64) | vs q8_0 |
|--------|---------------|---------|
| q8_0 | 31.04 tok/s | -- |
| turbo3_tcq | 30.04 tok/s | 97%!|(MISSING)
| turbo3 (scalar) | 30.04 tok/s | 97%!|(MISSING)

Decode speed is constant across context lengths (30 tok/s at 4K, 65K, and 128K). Prefill uses tensor-core MMA path at 99%!o(MISSING)f q8_0 speed.

## Custom codebooks

Trained codebooks are included in `codebooks/`. The defaults are compiled into the CUDA kernels, but you can override them:

```sh
TURBO_TCQ_CB=codebooks/3bit/product_aware_iter080.bin \
TURBO_TCQ_CB2=codebooks/2bit/product_aware_iter090.bin \
./build/bin/llama-server -m model.gguf -ngl 99 -fa \
  -ctk turbo3_tcq -ctv turbo3_tcq
```

Codebook training scripts are in `scripts/tcq_train_*.py`.

## How it works

1. **FWHT rotation** with random sign flips converts correlated KV vectors into i.i.d. Gaussian entries
2. **Viterbi encoding** on a 512-state (3-bit) or 256-state (2-bit) right-shift trellis finds the globally optimal codeword assignment
3. **O(1) sliding-window decode** -- each value decodes via a bit window lookup, no trellis traversal at inference
4. **Context-adaptive alpha** -- logarithmic norm scaling formula automatically adjusts dequantization scale per context length

## Supported models

Any GGUF model with `head_dim` that is a multiple of 128 works natively. Models with other head dimensions (e.g., Phi-3 at 96, Qwen3-0.6B at 64) are supported via automatic zero-padding.

Tested on: Qwen3.5-27B, Qwen3-32B, Gemma-3-27B, Gemma-4-31B, Harmonic-Hermes-9B, Phi-3-mini, and others.

---

## Quick start

Getting started with llama.cpp is straightforward. Here are several ways to install it on your machine:

- Install `llama.cpp` using [brew, nix or winget](docs/install.md)
- Run with Docker - see our [Docker documentation](docs/docker.md)
- Download pre-built binaries from the [releases page](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/releases)
- Build from source by cloning this repository - check out [our build guide](docs/build.md)

Once installed, you'll need a model to work with. Head to the [Obtaining and quantizing models](#obtaining-and-quantizing-models) section to learn more.

Example command:

```sh
# Use a local model file
llama-cli -m my_model.gguf

# Or download and run a model directly from Hugging Face
llama-cli -hf ggml-org/gemma-3-1b-it-GGUF

# Launch OpenAI-compatible API server
llama-server -hf ggml-org/gemma-3-1b-it-GGUF
```

## Description

The main goal of `llama.cpp` is to enable LLM inference with minimal setup and state-of-the-art performance on a wide
range of hardware - locally and in the cloud.

- Plain C/C++ implementation without any dependencies
- Apple silicon is a first-class citizen - optimized via ARM NEON, Accelerate and Metal frameworks
- AVX, AVX2, AVX512 and AMX support for x86 architectures
- RVV, ZVFH, ZFH, ZICBOP and ZIHINTPAUSE support for RISC-V architectures
- 1.5-bit, 2-bit, 3-bit, 4-bit, 5-bit, 6-bit, and 8-bit integer quantization for faster inference and reduced memory use
- Custom CUDA kernels for running LLMs on NVIDIA GPUs (support for AMD GPUs via HIP and Moore Threads GPUs via MUSA)
- Vulkan and SYCL backend support
- CPU+GPU hybrid inference to partially accelerate models larger than the total VRAM capacity

The `llama.cpp` project is the main playground for developing new features for the [ggml](https://github.com/ggml-org/ggml) library.

## Obtaining and quantizing models

The [Hugging Face](https://huggingface.co) platform hosts a [number of LLMs](https://huggingface.co/models?library=gguf&sort=trending) compatible with `llama.cpp`:

- [Trending](https://huggingface.co/models?library=gguf&sort=trending)
- [LLaMA](https://huggingface.co/models?sort=trending&search=llama+gguf)

You can either manually download the GGUF file or directly use any `llama.cpp`-compatible models from [Hugging Face](https://huggingface.co/) or other model hosting sites, by using this CLI argument: `-hf <user>/<model>[:quant]`. For example:

```sh
llama-cli -hf ggml-org/gemma-3-1b-it-GGUF
```

By default, the CLI would download from Hugging Face, you can switch to other options with the environment variable `MODEL_ENDPOINT`. The `MODEL_ENDPOINT` must point to a Hugging Face compatible API endpoint.

After downloading a model, use the CLI tools to run it locally - see below.

`llama.cpp` requires the model to be stored in the [GGUF](https://github.com/ggml-org/ggml/blob/master/docs/gguf.md) file format. Models in other data formats can be converted to GGUF using the `convert_*.py` Python scripts in this repo.

The Hugging Face platform provides a variety of online tools for converting, quantizing and hosting models with `llama.cpp`:

- Use the [GGUF-my-repo space](https://huggingface.co/spaces/ggml-org/gguf-my-repo) to convert to GGUF format and quantize model weights to smaller sizes
- Use the [GGUF-my-LoRA space](https://huggingface.co/spaces/ggml-org/gguf-my-lora) to convert LoRA adapters to GGUF format (more info: https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/discussions/10123)
- Use the [GGUF-editor space](https://huggingface.co/spaces/CISCai/gguf-editor) to edit GGUF meta data in the browser (more info: https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/discussions/9268)
- Use the [Inference Endpoints](https://ui.endpoints.huggingface.co/) to directly host `llama.cpp` in the cloud (more info: https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/discussions/9669)

To learn more about model quantization, [read this documentation](tools/quantize/README.md)

## [`llama-cli`](tools/cli)

#### A CLI tool for accessing and experimenting with most of `llama.cpp`'s functionality.

- <details open>
    <summary>Run in conversation mode</summary>

    Models with a built-in chat template will automatically activate conversation mode. If this doesn't occur, you can manually enable it by adding `-cnv` and specifying a suitable chat template with `--chat-template NAME`

    ```bash
    llama-cli -m model.gguf

    # > hi, who are you?
    # Hi there! I'm your helpful assistant! I'm an AI-powered chatbot designed to assist and provide information to users like you. I'm here to help answer your questions, provide guidance, and offer support on a wide range of topics. I'm a friendly and knowledgeable AI, and I'm always happy to help with anything you need. What's on your mind, and how can I assist you today?
    #
    # > what is 1+1?
    # Easy peasy! The answer to 1+1 is... 2!
    ```

    </details>

- <details>
    <summary>Run in conversation mode with custom chat template</summary>

    ```bash
    # use the "chatml" template (use -h to see the list of supported templates)
    llama-cli -m model.gguf -cnv --chat-template chatml

    # use a custom template
    llama-cli -m model.gguf -cnv --in-prefix 'User: ' --reverse-prompt 'User:'
    ```

    </details>

- <details>
    <summary>Constrain the output with a custom grammar</summary>

    ```bash
    llama-cli -m model.gguf -n 256 --grammar-file grammars/json.gbnf -p 'Request: schedule a call at 8pm; Command:'

    # {"appointmentTime": "8pm", "appointmentDetails": "schedule a a call"}
    ```

    The [grammars/](grammars/) folder contains a handful of sample grammars. To write your own, check out the [GBNF Guide](grammars/README.md).

    For authoring more complex JSON grammars, check out https://grammar.intrinsiclabs.ai/

    </details>


## [`llama-server`](tools/server)

#### A lightweight, [OpenAI API](https://github.com/openai/openai-openapi) compatible, HTTP server for serving LLMs.

- <details open>
    <summary>Start a local HTTP server with default configuration on port 8080</summary>

    ```bash
    llama-server -m model.gguf --port 8080

    # Basic web UI can be accessed via browser: http://localhost:8080
    # Chat completion endpoint: http://localhost:8080/v1/chat/completions
    ```

    </details>

- <details>
    <summary>Support multiple-users and parallel decoding</summary>

    ```bash
    # up to 4 concurrent requests, each with 4096 max context
    llama-server -m model.gguf -c 16384 -np 4
    ```

    </details>

- <details>
    <summary>Enable speculative decoding</summary>

    ```bash
    # the draft.gguf model should be a small variant of the target model.gguf
    llama-server -m model.gguf -md draft.gguf
    ```

    </details>

- <details>
    <summary>Serve an embedding model</summary>

    ```bash
    # use the /embedding endpoint
    llama-server -m model.gguf --embedding --pooling cls -ub 8192
    ```

    </details>

- <details>
    <summary>Serve a reranking model</summary>

    ```bash
    # use the /reranking endpoint
    llama-server -m model.gguf --reranking
    ```

    </details>

- <details>
    <summary>Constrain all outputs with a grammar</summary>

    ```bash
    # custom grammar
    llama-server -m model.gguf --grammar-file grammar.gbnf

    # JSON
    llama-server -m model.gguf --grammar-file grammars/json.gbnf
    ```

    </details>

    ```bash
    llama-perplexity -m model.gguf -f file.txt

    # [1]15.2701,[2]5.4007,[3]5.3073,[4]6.2965,[5]5.8940,[6]5.6096,[7]5.7942,[8]4.9297, ...
    # Final estimate: PPL = 5.4007 +/- 0.67339
    ```

    </details>

    <summary>Measure KL divergence</summary>


    </details>

## [`llama-bench`](tools/llama-bench)

    ```bash
    llama-bench -m model.gguf

    # Output:
    # | model               |       size |     params | backend    | threads |          test |                  t/s |
    # | ------------------- | ---------: | ---------: | ---------- | ------: | ------------: | -------------------: |
    # | qwen2 1.5B Q4_0     | 885.97 MiB |     1.54 B | Metal,BLAS |      16 |         pp512 |      5765.41 ± 20.55 |
    # | qwen2 1.5B Q4_0     | 885.97 MiB |     1.54 B | Metal,BLAS |      16 |         tg128 |        197.71 ± 0.81 |
    #
    # build: 3e0ba0e60 (4229)
    ```

    </details>

#### Seminal papers and background on the models

If your issue is with model generation quality, then please at least scan the following links and papers to understand the limitations of LLaMA models. This is especially important when choosing an appropriate model size and appreciating both the significant and subtle differences between LLaMA models and ChatGPT:
- LLaMA:
    - [Introducing LLaMA: A foundational, 65-billion-parameter large language model](https://ai.facebook.com/blog/large-language-model-llama-meta-ai/)
    - [LLaMA: Open and Efficient Foundation Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.13971)
- GPT-3
    - [Language Models are Few-Shot Learners](https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.14165)
- GPT-3.5 / InstructGPT / ChatGPT:
    - [Aligning language models to follow instructions](https://openai.com/research/instruction-following)
    - [Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.02155)

## XCFramework
The XCFramework is a precompiled version of the library for iOS, visionOS, tvOS,
and macOS. It can be used in Swift projects without the need to compile the
library from source. For example:
```swift
// swift-tools-version: 5.10
// The swift-tools-version declares the minimum version of Swift required to build this package.

import PackageDescription

let package = Package(
    name: "MyLlamaPackage",
    targets: [
        .executableTarget(
            name: "MyLlamaPackage",
            dependencies: [
                "LlamaFramework"
            ]),
        .binaryTarget(
            name: "LlamaFramework",
            url: "https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/releases/download/b5046/llama-b5046-xcframework.zip",
            checksum: "c19be78b5f00d8d29a25da41042cb7afa094cbf6280a225abe614b03b20029ab"
        )
    ]
)
```
The above example is using an intermediate build `b5046` of the library. This can be modified
to use a different version by changing the URL and checksum.

## Completions
Command-line completion is available for some environments.

#### Bash Completion
```bash
$ build/bin/llama-cli --completion-bash > ~/.llama-completion.bash
$ source ~/.llama-completion.bash
```
Optionally this can be added to your `.bashrc` or `.bash_profile` to load it
automatically. For example:
```console
$ echo "source ~/.llama-completion.bash" >> ~/.bashrc
```

## Dependencies

- [yhirose/cpp-httplib](https://github.com/yhirose/cpp-httplib) - Single-header HTTP server, used by `llama-server` - MIT license
- [stb-image](https://github.com/nothings/stb) - Single-header image format decoder, used by multimodal subsystem - Public domain
- [nlohmann/json](https://github.com/nlohmann/json) - Single-header JSON library, used by various tools/examples - MIT License
- [miniaudio.h](https://github.com/mackron/miniaudio) - Single-header audio format decoder, used by multimodal subsystem - Public domain
- [subprocess.h](https://github.com/sheredom/subprocess.h) - Single-header process launching solution for C and C++ - Public domain

# Llama.cpp Fork Upstream Sync

Systematic approach to synchronizing a monolithic llama.cpp fork with upstream while preserving fork-specific features (e.g., turboquant, TCQ, custom cache implementations).

## When to Use This Skill

- A fork (like buun-llama-cpp) has local modifications on top of llama.cpp but is **behind upstream**
- You need upstream fixes/optimizations while keeping fork-specific features
- The fork is monolithic (no submodules) — `ggml/` + `src/` + `tools/` all in one repo
- The fork has a `llama-upstream` remote pointing to `ggml-org/llama.cpp`

## Step 1: Analyze Fork State

### Check Remote Structure

```bash
cd ~/buun-llama-cpp
git remote -v
# Expected: origin (fork), llama-upstream (ggml-org), fork/myfork (personal)
```

### Determine Drift

```bash
git fetch llama-upstream origin
git rev-parse HEAD origin/master llama-upstream/master

# Commits ahead/behind
git rev-list --count llama-upstream/master..HEAD        # forks local commits on top of upstream
git rev-list --count HEAD..llama-upstream/master         # how far upstream has moved
git rev-list --count origin/master..llama-upstream/master  # drift since last sync
```

### Identify Fork's Unique Features

```bash
# Find where fork diverged from upstream
git merge-base origin/master llama-upstream/master

# View fork-only commits (the "unique" work)
git log --oneline llama-upstream/master..origin/master

# Categorize changes
git diff --stat llama-upstream/master..origin/master
# Look for: ggml/ changes, src/ changes, src/models/ additions, tools/ additions
```

### Key Questions

1. **How far behind upstream?** <50 commits → easy rebase. 100+ → complex.
2. **What are the fork's unique files?** (e.g., `turbo-rotation-data.h`, TCQ codebooks)
3. **Do upstream changes touch the same files?** → conflict risk
4. **Is there a recent merge commit?** → fork already attempted sync once

### Example: Buun's State (April 2026)

- Forked from upstream at commit `5d3a4a7da` ("server : fix logging of build + system info")
- Merged 88 upstream commits on 2026-04-05 (merge commit `6e07c8617`)
- Since merge: 181 commits ahead in upstream, 48 fork-specific commits
- Unique features: turbo2/3/4 TCQ, turbo rotation data, TCQ codebooks, turbo dequant
- Files changed vs upstream: 659 files (151 ggml/, 22 src/, 105 src/models/, 112 tools/)
- **Risk level**: MEDIUM (core ggml + src changes overlap)

## Step 2: Choose Sync Strategy

### Strategy A: Rebase (Recommended for moderate drift <100 commits)

Best when fork has few-to-moderate unique commits and you want clean linear history.

```bash
cd ~/buun-llama-cpp
git fetch llama-upstream
git rebase llama-upstream/master
# Resolve conflicts for each conflicting commit
# Then push: git push --force-with-lease fork master
```

**Pros:** Clean history, no merge commits
**Cons:** May need to re-resolve same conflicts on each sync. Rebase rewrites commit hashes.

### Strategy B: Merge with Conflict Resolution (For large drift)

Best when upstream has moved far and merge strategy automates bulk of resolution.

```bash
cd ~/buun-llama-cpp
git fetch llama-upstream
git merge llama-upstream/master -m "Merge upstream llamacpp/master (N commits)"
```

**Pros:** Preserves fork commits as separate history, easier to see what changed.
**Cons:** Creates merge commit, may need complex conflict resolution.

### Strategy C: Cherry-pick (For selective upstream changes)

Best when you only want specific upstream features, not everything.

```bash
cd ~/buun-llama-cpp
git fetch llama-upstream
# Cherry-pick specific commits
git cherry-pick COMMIT_HASH1 COMMIT_HASH2 ...
# Or cherry-pick a range
git cherry-pick BASE_HASH..llama-upstream/master
```

**Pros:** Complete control over what gets merged
**Cons:** Tedious for large numbers of commits, may miss transitive dependencies

## Step 3: Conflict Resolution Framework

When conflicts arise, use this decision tree:

### File Categories and Resolution Priority

| File Category | Priority | Resolution Rule |
|---------------|----------|-----------------|
| `ggml/src/ggml-cuda/*.cu` | HIGH | Keep upstream (CUDA is actively maintained) |
| `ggml/src/ggml-cpu/*.c` | HIGH | Keep upstream (CPU kernels are upstream-led) |
| `src/models/model*.cpp` | HIGH | Keep upstream (model support is upstream-driven) |
| `src/llama-*.cpp` (core) | HIGH | Keep upstream unless fork has a documented fix |
| `ggml/` (tensor ops) | HIGH | Keep upstream |
| `turbo-rotation-data.h` | **FORK** | Keep fork (unique to buun's turbo features) |
| `turbo-rotation-data-32.h` | **FORK** | Keep fork |
| `*tcq*` files | **FORK** | Keep fork (TCQ is buun's signature feature) |
| `codebooks/*.bin` | **FORK** | Keep fork (training artifacts) |
| `turbo*.h` / `turbo*.cpp` | **FORK** | Keep fork |
| `CMakeLists.txt` (root) | MEDIUM | Merge both, check options |
| `cmake/*.cmake` | MEDIUM | Merge both unless conflicting |
| `tools/server/*.cpp` | MEDIUM | Keep upstream unless fork has server fix |
| `.devops/*.Dockerfile` | MEDIUM | Keep upstream |
| `.github/*.yml` | LOW | Keep upstream (CI is standardized) |
| `README.md` | LOW | Keep upstream |

### Common Conflict Patterns

#### Pattern 1: Upstream added a file the fork already has
```
# Upstream adds ggml/src/ggml-cuda/kernel_x.cuh
# Buun also has ggml/src/ggml-cuda/kernel_x.cuh (with turbo additions)
```
**Resolution:** Merge both — keep upstream's general implementation + buun's turbo-specific parts.

#### Pattern 2: Upstream renamed a file/function
```
# Upstream: llama-kv-cache.cpp → llama-cache.cpp
# Buun: still uses llama-kv-cache.cpp with turbo additions
```
**Resolution:** Keep fork naming for now, rebase later when upstream naming stabilizes.

#### Pattern 3: Upstream changed a struct the fork extends
```
# Upstream: struct llama_kv_cache { ... }
# Buun: struct llama_kv_cache { ... + turbo_fields; }
```
**Resolution:** Merge both — upstream's struct changes go in, fork's turbo fields added after.

#### Pattern 4: Upstream removed a feature the fork depends on
```
# Upstream removed feature X
# Buun's turbo features depend on feature X
```
**Resolution:** This is a **BREAKING CHANGE** — assess whether upstream's replacement feature achieves the same goal. If not, fork may be broken.

#### Pattern 5: Build system changes
```
# Upstream: CMakeLists.txt uses new cmake policies
# Buun: CMakeLists.txt has custom options
```
**Resolution:** Keep upstream cmake changes, add fork-specific options on top.

## Step 4: Post-Sync Verification

### Build Test

```bash
cd ~/buun-llama-cpp
rm -rf build && mkdir build && cd build
cmake .. -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release -DGGML_CUDA=ON
make -j$(nproc) 2>&1 | tee build.log
# Check for errors
grep -i "error" build.log
```

### Feature Test

```bash
# Verify fork features still work
./build/bin/llama-cli -m model.gguf \
  --cache-type-k turbo2 \
  --cache-type-v turbo2 \
  --cache-disk-persistent 2>&1 | head -20
```

### Commit Log Check

```bash
# After successful sync, verify clean history
git log --oneline -20
# Should show merge commit + clean fork commits above it
```

## Step 5: Push and Document

### Push Changes

```bash
# After successful sync
git push fork master  # Or: git push --force-with-lease fork master (if rebased)

# Tag the sync
git tag -a sync-YYYYMMDD -m "Sync with upstream master as of YYYY-MM-DD"
```

### Update Memory

Record the sync state for future reference:
- Fork commit hash
- Upstream commit hash
- Date of sync
- Conflicts resolved
- Any features broken

## Decision Summary

```
How far behind upstream?
├── < 30 commits → Rebase (easy, low conflict risk)
├── 30-100 commits → Rebase with conflict resolution (medium)
├── 100-300 commits → Rebase with merge strategy (hard, expect conflicts)
└── 300+ commits → Consider cherry-picking selectively or re-evaluating fork viability
```

**Rule of thumb:** Rebase is preferred for maintaining clean history. Merge is acceptable when the fork has been in use and rebasing would disrupt active development.

## Related Skills

- **llama-cpp-fork-evaluation** — Evaluate forks before integration
- **llama-cpp-autonomous-update** — Autonomous update for upstream (not forked) repos
- **llama-cpp** — General llama.cpp usage

# Buun-Llama Daily Sync

Automated daily sync of the Buun-llama fork with both upstream sources.

## When to Use This Skill

- Running as a cron job (daily) to keep ~/buun-llama-cpp up to date
- The llama-server needs the latest TurboQuant/TCQ features for model inference
- Keep gkraker04/buun-llama-cpp synced with spiritbuun/buun-llama-cpu

## Repository Structure

```
~/buun-llama-cpp/
  remotes:
    origin      -> https://github.com/spiritbuun/buun-llama-cpp.git  (Buun's fork, upstream)
    llama-upstream -> https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp.git       (official upstream)
    fork        -> https://github.com/gkraker04/buun-llama-cpp.git   (our GitHub fork)
```

## Procedure

### Step 1: Fetch Latest from Both Upstreams

```bash
cd ~/buun-llama-cpp
git fetch origin master llama-upstream master fork/master 2>&1
```

### Step 2: Check Divergence

```bash
# Current state
CURRENT=$(git rev-parse HEAD)
BUUN_LATEST=$(git rev-parse origin/master)
LLAMA_CPP_LATEST=$(git rev-parse llama-upstream/master)
FORK_LATEST=$(git rev-parse fork/master)

echo "HEAD: $CURRENT"
echo "Buun-llama latest: $BUUN_LATEST"
echo "ggml-org/llama.cpp latest: $LLAMA_CPP_LATEST"
echo "fork/master: $FORK_LATEST"

# Check if Buun has new commits
NEW_BUUN=$(git rev-list --count HEAD..origin/master)
echo "New Buun commits: $NEW_BUUN"

# Check how far behind ggml-org/llama.cpp we are (Buun-specific features beyond upstream)
BUUN_UNIQUE=$(git rev-list --count llama-upstream/master..origin/master)
echo "Buun-llama unique commits beyond ggml-org: $BUUN_UNIQUE"
```

### Step 3: Determine Action Needed

| Scenario | Action |
|----------|--------|
| HEAD == origin/master (no new Buun commits) | Nothing to do, skip sync |
| HEAD behind origin/master by N commits | Merge latest Buun-llama into HEAD |
| Origin has conflicts with llama-upstream | Need merge conflict resolution |

### Step 4: Sync (if needed)

```bash
cd ~/buun-llama-cpp

# Create temporary sync branch
git checkout -b temp-sync origin/master

# Attempt merge with llama-upstream if Buun is behind ggml-org
BUUN_AT=$(git rev-parse HEAD)
UPSTREAM_AT=$(git rev-parse llama-upstream/master)

if [ "$BUUN_AT" != "$(git merge-base $BUUN_AT $UPSTREAM_AT | awk '{print $1}')" ]; then
    echo "Buun-llama not at ggml-org head, checking for safe merge..."
    # Only attempt merge if no conflicting files exist in Buun's unique commits
    CONFLICTS=$(git diff --name-only llama-upstream/master..origin/master | \
        grep -vE "(turbo|tcq|codebook)" | wc -l)
    
    if [ "$CONFLICTS" -gt 20 ]; then
        echo "WARNING: $CONFLICTS non-turbo files differ, merge may fail"
        # Safe path: stay on origin/master (Buun-llama already has merged features)
    else
        git merge llama-upstream/master --no-edit 2>&1
    fi
fi

# Build test
rm -rf build && mkdir build && cd build
cmake .. -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release \
    -DGGML_CUDA=ON \
    -DGGML_NATIVE=ON \
    -DGGML_CUDA_FA=ON \
    -DGGML_CUDA_FA_ALL_QUANTS=ON 2>&1 | tail -5
make -j4 2>&1 | tail -10

# Check for errors
if grep -qi "error" build.log 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "BUILD FAILED — aborting sync"
    exit 1
fi

# Push to fork if changes detected
SYNC_HEAD=$(git rev-parse HEAD)
if [ "$SYNC_HEAD" != "$FORK_LATEST" ]; then
    git push fork HEAD:master 2>&1 | tail -5
    echo "Pushed to fork/master: $SYNC_HEAD"
else
    echo "No sync needed — already at latest"
fi
```

### Step 5: Report Results

Summarize what happened:
- New Buun commits pulled (count)
- Build result (PASS/FAIL)  
- Push result (new SHA)

## Known Issues & Caveats

1. **Buun's TurboQuant code is already merged** — The fork at `origin/master` already includes 188 unique Buun-specific commits beyond ggml-org/llama.cpp. Most of these are TurboQuant features already present in the build.

2. **ggml-org files that differ from Buun-llama** — There are ~54 files in ggml-org/llama.cpp not in Buun-llama (newer upstream features). These should NOT be blindly merged as they may break TurboQuant code. Only merge if no conflicts exist with TurboQuant-specific files.

3. **Build is the final authority** — If the build compiles, the sync succeeded. If it fails, roll back to last known good state.

4. **Never kill :8081 llama-server** during this process — The server must remain running for inference traffic. Only rebuild if absolutely necessary.

## Daily Cron Integration

This skill is intended to run via cron:
```
# Every day at 3 AM
0 3 * * * cd ~/buun-llama-cpp && ~/hermes/hermes -c "Run the buun-llama-daily-sync skill"
```

The cron job should:
1. Mount I: drive if needed (WSL)
2. Run this sync procedure
3. Log results to ~/.hermes/llama-server/buun-sync.log
4. Alert only on build failure or push error

## Related Skills

- `llama-cpp-upstream-sync` — General fork sync methodology
- `llama-cpp-autonomous-update` — Autonomous upstream updates (non-fork)
- `llama-server-lifecycle-management` — Managing llama-server uptime during updates
"}
